attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the Labour and Delivery unit at 38 weeks gestation with a six-hour history of reduced fetal movements. This is her first pregnancy and she has no significant medical history. She does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The fundal height is appropriate for gestational age. A cardiotocograph (CTG) is performed, which shows a baseline fetal heart rate of 140 beats per minute with reduced variability. Repetitive decelerations are noted, with the onset occurring after the peak of the contraction and a slow return to baseline.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the type of decelerations seen on this CTG?
attributes.explanation:
The cardiotocograph (CTG) described in this scenario shows late decelerations, which are a concerning sign of fetal compromise. Late decelerations are characterized by their timing in relation to uterine contractions: they begin after the peak of the contraction and have a slow return to baseline. This pattern is indicative of uteroplacental insufficiency and fetal hypoxia.

Early decelerations are typically benign and associated with head compression during contractions. They mirror the contraction pattern, starting and ending with the contraction. This pattern does not match the description given in the stem.

Variable decelerations are associated with cord compression. They have a rapid onset and recovery, and their shape and timing can vary from contraction to contraction. The decelerations described in the stem have a consistent pattern, ruling out variable decelerations.

Prolonged decelerations last for more than 2 minutes but less than 10 minutes. While concerning, they do not match the repetitive pattern described in relation to contractions in this scenario.

Sinusoidal pattern is a rare but ominous finding characterized by a smooth, sine wave-like undulating pattern without normal beat-to-beat variability. This does not match the description given in the stem.

The presence of late decelerations, especially in the context of reduced fetal movements, is a significant concern. It suggests that the fetus is not tolerating labour well and may be experiencing hypoxia. This finding, combined with the history of reduced fetal movements, warrants immediate intervention, which may include intrauterine resuscitation measures (such as maternal repositioning, intravenous fluids, and oxygen administration) and preparation for possible emergency delivery if the fetal status does not improve.

It's important to note that the interpretation of CTG should always be done in the context of the full clinical picture, including maternal and fetal risk factors, stage of labour, and other CTG features such as baseline rate, variability, and presence of accelerations.
learningPoints: Late decelerations on cardiotocography (CTG) are indicative of uteroplacental insufficiency and fetal hypoxia.
Reduced fetal movements can be a sign of fetal compromise and warrant immediate assessment.
The presence of late decelerations requires prompt intervention to prevent adverse fetal outcomes.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Intrapartum fetal surveillance: clinical guideline. 5th ed. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2021. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Intrapartum-Fetal-Surveillance.pdf
